data.title: TCW Durable Growth ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

GRW is an actively managed fund focused on companies with market leadership positions and proven track records of resilient, predictable growth and significant free cash flow generation. Our thesis is that investing in businesses with durable competitive advantages, essential or hard-to-replicate products or services and exceptionally skilled management teams will drive significant compounding performance over the long term. Through GRW, investors can invest in and benefit from the robust performance generated by these exceptionally high-quality companies.

Performance

Net of fees, GRW delivered a return of -8.25% in Q3 2025, compared to the S&P 500 Index's return of 8.12%. Since its inception in January 2016, GRW has delivered a cumulative return of 334.89% compared to the 308.23% return for the S&P 500 Index over the same period.

While GRW performed well in the first half of 2025, the third quarter proved challenging for the strategy with the market recovery after the Liberation Day sell off driven by high beta(more volatile) companies. GRW focuses on lower beta companies with durable business models and significant recurring revenue, which contributed to this quarter's underperformance. While the quarter was frustrating, we can draw upon roughly a decade of managing this concentrated portfolio which is fundamentals-driven and does not chase the market. That experience has taught us that our investments go through cycles- sometimes in favor, sometimes out of favor- which can cause meaningful performance dislocations. We count 13 such drawdowns versus the market, or about once every 10 months. In 11 of those 13 instances, GRW went on to outperform the market materially over the following 6- and 12-month periods. This fact pattern combined with the attractiveness of the fundamentals and valuations of the portfolio give us confidence that the Fund is very well positioned going forward.

The top performers in the quarter were GE Aerospace (GE), O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY), and Cadence Design Systems (CDNS).

GE shares continued their strong performance as the aerospace aftermarket trends continue to be robust, supported by the aging global airline fleet and higher defense spending in the U.S. and Europe. Early in the quarter, the company reported stellar earnings, beating expectations at both the top and bottom lines, and GE also lifted full-year guidance. Management cited robust commercial services as a key driver in the quarter.

O'Reilly shares posted a strong quarter with the company able to pass along incremental tariffs without issue. The company also raised 2025 revenue and same store sales guidance, indicating that demand has remained healthy.

Cadence Design Systems surged in July after reported second quarter earnings that beat expectations across the board. Management also raised third quarter and full-year guidance, with Cadence continuing to benefit from robust AI-driven demand for its electronic design automation software.

The primary detractors in the quarter were Constellation Software (CSU:CA), Fiserv (FI)(FISV), and McGraw Hill (MH).

Constellation Software shares fell in September after news broke that founder and President, Mark Leonard would be resigning due to health concerns. Leonard has been critical in shaping the company's successful strategy of acquiring and growing mission-critical software companies serving niche markets, though he has not been highly involved in the day-to-day operations of CSU for a decade. We believe the company is in good hands with incoming CEO Mark Miller, who has been with the company since inception in 1995 and was previously the COO. Mark brings a similar level of rigor and focus on the company's culture of excellence, talent development, and M&A process.

Fiserv shares underperformed with continued market rotation out of lower beta companies, such as FI, and into higher beta companies. Additionally, there are concerns about the ramp to mid-teens second half growth in FI's Merchant business required to achieve the 10% 2025 organic revenue growth guidance. While we acknowledge that the merchant growth could be difficult to achieve, this is well known by the market, and we believe the company is significantly undervalued relative to its long-term free cash flow growth.

McGraw Hill shares struggled following their IPO in July on concerns about AI posing a risk to the business model over the longer term. We note that we exited the position.

Market Commentary

The three major indices (S&P 500, Dow Jones, and Nasdaq) each closed the quarter near all-time highs despite economic growth concerns, a softening labor market, elevated inflation, and tariff implementation. All sectors of the S&P 500 appreciated in the quarter except for Consumer Staples, although year-to-date through the end of September, all are in positive territory. The performance is quite impressive following the broad selloff we saw following Trump's “Liberation Day” in early April.

Through the quarter tariff-related concerns dominated headlines especially in August when President Trump signed an Executive Order raising the rate against Canadian goods to 35% from 25 and the announcement of a 90-day pause on tariff implementation with Mexico after making agreements with many trade partners including Japan, U.K., Vietnam, Indonesia, and the Philippines. As we rolled into the end of the summer, tariff headlines have all but been forgotten, save for lone headlines of notable developments or forward-looking mentions of potential inflation.

Both growth and inflation for the second quarter came in higher than expected suggesting a resilient U.S. economy and consumer that has thus far weathered the impact from higher tariffs. After a mild contraction in 1Q, the latest 2Q GDP read came in at 3.8% led by strong consumer spending and a noticeable decline in imports due in no small part to the implementation and/or increase in tariffs.

As anticipated, the Fed delivered a 25-basis point rate cut and signaled potential for two more cuts by year-end sending major indices, including the S&P 500, NASDAQ, and Russell 2000, to new all-time highs. Meanwhile, the re-emergence of tariff threats and the reality of a government shutdown, served as reminders of headline risk, even if investors treated them more as background noise than as drivers of fundamentals.

Looking forward, we expect the equity market to remain data dependent, with investor attention increasingly focused on whether earnings growth in 2026 can sustain current valuation levels. At roughly 23x forward earnings, multiples remain elevated by historical standards and leave little margin for disappointment. To justify these valuations, profit growth and free cash flow delivery will need to do more of the heavy lifting, particularly in a slower growth environment. There are many constructive dynamics supporting the equity market, including generally healthy corporate balance sheets, ample liquidity, and powerful secular growth drivers such as artificial intelligence, digital infrastructure, and the energy transition. At the same time, we are mindful of risks, including policy uncertainty, tariff disputes, and the potential for fiscal disruptions, all of which could unsettle sentiment at the margin. Against this backdrop, our focus remains on companies with durable business models, recurring free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation, as well as those positioned to benefit from the earnings growth prospects associated with long-duration secular themes. In our view, these characteristics offer the best potential for compounding value in an environment where earnings delivery, rather than further multiple expansion, is likely to be the key driver of equity returns.

data.title: ETJ's Defensive Promise Vs. Actual Performance: A Hold Rating
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Risk-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETJ) is a option protected active portfolio with a focus on income. The yields are attractive and it could do very well in limiting drawdowns because of its defensive option structure. However, the option costs add up over time and work out only to an extent as seen in past performance data. Its risk-reward balance should pay off in extreme crashes or drawdowns, but there are alternatives today that employ covered calls and are performing far better. I think ETJ is a Hold because of its risk mitigation positioning. But its actual drawdown performance and quite a few alternatives that have emerged in the past few years, prevents it from being rated a Buy.

A Methodology Discussion - What to Expect

ETJ buys protective broader index puts (S&P 500) on a portfolio of active holdings. These puts are insurances against deep corrections and are typically placed some distance away from current levels. As of September end 2025, the average put indicates a ~5% OTM strike with a good portfolio coverage of ~97%. These are typically for near term expiries with lower time values that make it less expensive as a hedge.

ETJ also sells calls on the portfolio (again S&P 500 calls) that appear to be relatively closer to the money with similar coverage and expiry characteristics, like that of the bought puts. The option layer is actively managed, so it may change depending on market conditions, but the September positions indicate tight coverage (near full portfolio coverage). This implies drawdowns should be capped and so will be upsides. However, since the individual stocks are not hedged through their own options (but a broader S&P 500 option layer is introduced), there could be some amount of upside opportunity and larger drawdown possibilities due to the stocks' own idiosyncratic risk-return profiles. But still, investor should broadly expect capped drawdowns and limited upsides.

The strikes chosen could also vary, and September end positions are not conclusive (as markets move after initial positions). However, even if the strikes are equidistant, puts could be costlier than calls in most cases (volatility smirk), which more than erases the credit earned through call selling. The strategy could end up being net credit positive depending on its exact implementation (such as closer strikes for sold calls or more portfolio coverage of sold calls), but the net effect is marginal. The September positions with calls written ATM and puts ~5% OTM, for example, could be a net credit setup, if they were the same positions when the options were setup initially.

All in all, the ETJ setup seems to aim to derive minor net credit from the option play and gains from active bets of the underlying portfolio. The primary aim is to reduce volatility and force the NAV to exhibit a lower fluctuation profile than the broader markets.

This is slightly contrasting to some of the more recent evolution in the covered call income ETF space where protective puts are mostly missing. Strategies like XYLD or SPYI, or even more recently GPIX aim to reduce the drawdowns by a more limited degree - only offset by premiums earned through various degrees of call writing. The lack of a defensive put buy position also means there is more income to be earned if the setup works out (net credit). These covered calls also cap upside, but to a lesser degree.

Overall, investors in ETJ should expect a far lower volatility of the underlying holdings' total returns. However, theoretically, SPYI and similar ETFs could earn decent income even in flat markets because of their net credit positions. I have summarized expectations from the two different styles of option plays in the below table. There are several assumptions in the numerical examples (plus active calls take by the portfolio management on exact option setup that could impact performance), but it should directionally help understand the upside capping and downside expectations from the two different approaches.

What Actually Transpires

Actual performances do not show what theory indicates. I have plotted the total returns of all the relevant ETFs and ETJ (along with SPY as a proxy for the S&P 500) below. ETJ seems to capture upsides reasonably well compared to the covered call strategies, and despite the costs of a put hedge on the downside. This can be due to implementation specifics of an active option setup, but I was still expecting lower upside capture capabilities from ETJ. However, it is also noted that ETJ has not been able to set itself up as well in the rebound from the April 2025 sell-off (still holds up well compared to most of the covered call plays that should capture upsides better).

What I expect ETJ to do better is prevent sizeable drawdowns, limiting it versus the covered call strategy's limited protection. Even there ETJ surprises my theoretical expectations. In April, when markets fell by ~17%, ETJ does better than all of the covered call plays as expected. But I was expecting a capped drawdown at ~5% because of the put hedges. That is not seen. ETJ goes down by almost ~12.5%. This deviation from expectation, seen both in terms of upside capturing and drawdown mitigation, could happen because of the option setup specifics. Or the underlying portfolio.

The Portfolio as Alpha

ETJ's portfolio size is only ~50 compared to the underlying portfolios for the covered call ETF pack (typically close to the 500 mark for the S&P 500). Despite the smaller portfolio, the top 10 allocation in ETJ is not far more concentrated than SPY. ETJ has a ~48% weight in the top 10 compared to SPY's 40% - concentration levels not commensurate to the portfolio size.

ETJ is systematically overweight most mega-cap techs vs SPY. Also some other active names pop up in the top 10 for ETJ like Eli Lilly and Coca Cola, that are not seen in SPY's top 10. Overall, there are differences, but the approach is not broadly different - there is a market cap themed active portfolio in ETJ that broadly follows the S&P 500 base weights. But it reduces the size of the portfolio and becomes overweight in mega caps and introduces some active bets, which are also heavy weights in the S&P 500 if not in the top 10.

Even the sectoral allocations show only minor deviations between ETJ and SPY. ETJ is slightly overweight in tech and communications (Google, Meta, Amazon), but directionally retains the sectoral flavor of the S&P 500. Overall, there is potential for alpha in the ETJ portfolio, but still highly correlated. ETJ does not have a completely different portfolio where the alpha generation can directionally overturn SPY's performance to a large extent.

Summary of Discussion

The combination of ETJ's active portfolio and option setup leads me to believe that it captures upsides relatively better than expected and even matches most of the covered call pack. But this is not consistent, as seen since April 2025.

On the other hand, ETJ's primary advantage over the covered calls should have been the limited drawdowns. That does not happen to the degree expected. Since ETJ seems to pose a similar degree of drawdowns (with minor improvements), I see more value in a proposition like SPYI or GPIX where the upside capture is at least more consistent and which has the theoretical advantage of generating income even in flat market conditions (refer to comparison table).

ETJ is not a poor fund, but its primary drawdown objective is not seen consistently enough. The fact that it is trading at a 10% discount to NAV makes its case lucrative. But overall returns over time have not been consistently better than SPYI or GPIX. I see ETJ bordering on a Buy on performance grounds, but its performance seems to deviate from the offering's lower volatility promise (compared to covered call strategies). That plus recent underperformance makes its case a Hold till more performance data emerges. Keep ETJ in the mix for its promise in deep crashes, but recent empirical evidence suggests there may be better value in covered calls than this fund.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: Still A Natural Gas Producer
data.description:
Tourmaline Oil (OTCPK:TRMLF) has long been one of the largest natural gas producers in Canada. Recently management touted the increase in liquids production of 4% in the third quarter earnings press release. This was the growth over the second quarter (as opposed to comparing the production to the same quarter of the previous fiscal year). But for such a small part of production, that is not really the kind of growth that would materially change the production mix for what is essentially a natural gas producer with some liquids production.

Earnings Summary

Tourmaline is large enough to keep natural gas prices in relatively good shape despite the AECO volatility that has been reported by many other companies that I follow.

Even though the last article first covered management's goal to grow liquids production. As the comparison above demonstrates, liquids production really did not grow more than natural gas production. In fact, liquids production grew more slowly than natural gas production when compared to the previous fiscal year.

Most likely this is due to the company's overall exposure to some very good geography for natural gas production. Even though cash flow per share declined, that cash flow is darn good considering the AECO volatility in the third quarter (that saw AECO prices actually go negative for a bit).

This also points out the fact that even though the company announced a goal to grow AECO production, industry conditions will likely rule drilling decisions. This company has some very profitable natural gas acreage. That acreage will not disappear from consideration just because the company has announced that liquids will grow. As a result, this company will likely remain a natural gas producer with a slowly increasing liquids production percentage over time.

Like many decisions made by management, the decision to increase liquids production will be based upon relative profitability. The increasing ability to export natural gas may strengthen natural gas prices to the point where such a goal is not realistic for the company.

There is also the priority to bring natural gas production online for the important heating season. The strong natural gas prices combined with the high initial production of new wells often adds a couple of percentage points to the estimated ROI for the wells drilled.

What may happen instead is there are other parts of the year where it may be a profit maximizing decision to emphasize the rich gas production that would increase the percentage of liquids in the production mix. So, we will likely have to wait and see how the execution of the goal mentioned in the last article actually happens.

What may be more material is that the relatively strong natural gas price realized in a seasonally weak quarter like the third quarter may well portend a very strong fourth quarter unless the winter turns out to be unseasonably warm.

Polar Vortex

In fact, the polar vortex is weakening which could mean a colder than expected period. Keep in mind that the weather is a very low visibility discussion. Nonetheless, the possibility of some really cold weather is growing for those that watch the Polar Vortex and its potential effects.

La Ninas are known for actually warm weather without considering the cold air leaks that seem to happen a lot during La Ninas. It is these cold air "leaks" (for lack of a better term) that give La Ninas their cold reputation.

One of these "leaks" is caused by a breakdown of the Polar Vortex that often happens during a La Nina. This causes unusually cold air to go further South than it might. That seems to be a potential threat that is developing now. If it happens, it would be very good for this company and the natural gas industry in general.

The market may well be responding to this event as it unfolds and the chances for a very cold period either increase or decline depending upon that unfolding.

Outspending Cash Flow

The important heating season is a reason that many natural gas producers will outspend cash flow just before heating season gets underway.

As shown above, this management has done what most of the Canadian industry does. But note that for the year-to-date, capital expenditures are less than cash flow.

There are quarters like the second quarter which has traditionally low activity levels because of Spring Breakup. It also may not be worth it to bring online a lot of production in the first quarter because the first quarter marks the end (for the most part) of the strong heating season prices. Canada is still cold in April. But the end of the bulk of the winter heating season is in view at that point.

Summary

Tourmaline Oil appears to be in an excellent position to report a strong fourth quarter. A breakdown of the Polar Vortex could provide a boost to that optimistic scene. But it may not be necessary.

This is an investment grade idea that is likely to do well no matter what happens the next few years because liquids production is becoming increasingly important to the company.

Commodity prices and relative profitability of various locations under different industry conditions will likely determine what the production mix is some years from now. This is a very low visibility industry. Therefore, it could take some time for that goal to increase liquids production as a percentage of the mix to actually happen. Goals in this industry tend to be very flexible.

A large upstream producer that is seen as investment grade tends to be a bit safer than some of the smaller producers that I follow. It has more bargaining power to receive better prices for its production than its smaller competitors.

On the other hand, it tends to grow slower. Any upstream producer needs to be viewed as a variable distribution entity due to the reliance on commodity prices for the distribution. Nonetheless, the base distribution is likely more defensible than is the case where some producers just pay out what they can when they can. As such, this is likely a strong buy cyclical growth and income idea.

Risks

Future commodity prices are extremely low visibility and volatile. A material and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company.

Michael Rose, CEO and Chairman, has been the major driver of this company since its establishment. A lack of his services for any reason could result in a material change in the company's future outlook.

When the time comes for the next generation of managers to take over, succession in a company like Tourmaline is often a very risky process. It is not unusual for there to be several management turnovers until the correct new management is found.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Brewing U.S. Relief With Brazil Beans
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump drops 40% tariffs on Brazilian beef, coffee amid rising food prices. (00:22) Joby Aviation (JOBY) accuses rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) of corporate espionage. (01:26) PepsiCo (PEP) preps for its prebiotic drink launch. (02:16)

This is an abridged transcript.

President Trump has signed an order to lift 40% tariffs on certain agricultural products from Brazil, including coffee, beef, fruits and cocoa expanding efforts to bring down rising food prices in the U.S.

Trump said the order was due to progress in negotiations with Brazil and various officials recommending the move.

The order applies to Brazilian imports to the U.S. on or after Nov. 13, and may require refunds of duties already collected.

The move follows a rollback of tariffs on several agricultural products to help ease high grocery prices for American consumers.

Brazil has faced 50% tariffs for months. Trump raised them beyond his 10% across-the-board tariffs to penalize Brazil for prosecuting his ally Jair Bolsonaro, the country's former president.

To note, Brazil is the world's leading coffee exporter and a third of the coffee beans in the U.S. comes from the Latin American country. It is also the world's top beef exporter and a key beef supplier for burgers.

Joby Aviation (JOBY) has filed a lawsuit against rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) in a California Superior Court in Santa Cruz, alleging that confidential information taken by a former employee who went to work for Archer (ACHR) was used to disrupt Joby’s exclusive client partnership.

According to court documents, Joby’s (JOBY) then U.S. state and local policy head, George Kivork, had access to confidential and proprietary information regarding Joby’s strategic partnerships, technical information, and sensitive information that would be valuable to a competitor.

Two days prior to announcing his resignation, Joby (JOBY) alleges Kivork exfiltrated a cache of “highly valuable Joby files.” Less than three weeks later, Joby (JOBY) was notified by a strategic partner that they were approached by Archer (ACHR) with detailed knowledge about the confidential terms of that partner’s agreement with Joby (JOBY).

“This is corporate espionage, planned and premeditated,” Joby states in its lawsuit.

In a statement to CNBC, Archer’s (ACHR) chief legal officer called Joby’s (JOBY) allegations “without merit,” and the complaint “does not identify a single specific trade secret, let alone any evidence of misappropriation.”

A hearing is scheduled for March 20, 2026.

PepsiCo (PEP) is unveiling its latest innovation in the soda category next week. Pepsi Prebiotic Cola will launch initially as an online exclusive for Black Friday before rolling out more broadly in 2026.

The introduction of Pepsi Prebiotic Cola comes on the heels of PepsiCo’s major $1.95 billion acquisition of Poppi, an independent prebiotic soda company.

Pepsi Prebiotic Cola will be offered in Original and Cherry Vanilla. Each 12-ounce can contains 3 grams of prebiotic fiber to support gut health, with only 5 grams of cane sugar and 30 calories per can. The beverage line will have no artificial sweeteners. The move reflects PepsiCo's (PEP) broad strategy to market a lower-sugar, clean-label alternative in response to evolving wellness trends. Company officials called it the boldest evolution in cola in more than two decades.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Wall Street's slide likely driven by signals in crypto - Fundstrat's Lee, IBKR's Sosnick

Nvidia-Foxconn's Taiwan supercomputing center to be ready by H1 2026

Strategy may face billions in outflows if excluded from MSCI indices - JPMorgan

Catalyst watch:

  • Universal Pictures' (CMCSA) Wicked: For Good will open in U.S. theaters. The film has generated strong advance ticket sales, with Fandango calling it the biggest pre-seller for a PG film in their history.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is down 2.6% at $57/barrel. Bitcoin is down 4.7% at $82,000. Gold is down 1% at $4,033.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.5% and the DAX is down 1.1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: The Gap (GAP) +6% - Shares jumped after beating Q3 expectations, with comparable sales rising for the seventh straight quarter and hitting their highest level since January 2018, outperforming estimates by nearly 2 pts.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 7:30 am New York Federal Reserve Bank President John Williams gives keynote before the Annual Conference of the Central Bank of Chile.

  • 9:00 am Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan participates in a moderated panel before the"SNB and its Watchers 2025" event.

  • 9:00 am Boston Federal Reserve Bank President Susan Collins is scheduled to deliver opening remarks and appear in a televised interview at the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston.

And today is news quiz day. I’ll leave a link in the show notes section.

data.title: NNN REIT: Ignore The Short-Term Noise And Buy This Well-Covered Yield With Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

NNN REIT (NNN) is a stock that I've been bullish on for quite some time despite their ongoing headwinds. In their most recent Q3, the REIT reported what I thought was a solid earnings report despite their share price declining.

While their headwinds are likely to continue near-term, limiting any significant upside, I remain bullish on the stock long-term due to their astute management, well-covered yield, and upside potential over the next year or two.

In this article, I'll discuss NNN's latest earnings, what's caused their price pullback, and why I remain bullish on this Dividend Aristocrat despite lingering headwinds.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered NNN REIT in an article back in September titled: Quality Speaks For Itself, And The 3.4% Dividend Raise Amidst Tenant Headwinds Is Proof.

Despite tenant headwinds, NNN was able to show growth in core FFO, AFFO, and revenue. Management also raised full-year guidance and their dividend, extending their streak to 26 years of consecutive dividend raises.

Their credit quality remained solid. And at a forward P/AFFO multiple of 12.35x and below its peers, upside to their end of 2027 price target of $56.44 were reasons for long-term investors to buy.

Since, the REIT has continued to see slight volatility, down an additional 4.19%. This is in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up a little over 5% at the time of writing.

What's Going On With NNN?

I've touched on NNN's lingering tenant problems, particularly from tenants At Home, who filed for bankruptcy earlier this year, Frisch's Big Boy, and Badcock Home Furniture.

While the REIT continues to work through current issues, their occupancy experienced a steep decline in their latest quarter.

During Q3, NNN saw occupancy tick down to 97.5%. Year-over-year, this was a sizable decline from 99.3%. And also, the likely reason the stock has been somewhat volatile.

But I view the decline in occupancy and expect this to rise in the near-term as management works to get these resolved. Management attributed the decline to legal dispute between their new & former tenants resulted in the REIT parting ways due to legal uncertainty.

And this caused the occupancy decline. But management remains confident in resolving this issue by year's end.

During the quarter, they sold and re-leased 15 of the 64 vacant assets. And expect to sell an additional 14 by the first quarter of 2026. Management occupancy to rise above 98% by year's end.

If NNN is able to successfully re-tenant their vacant properties, I expect price appreciation to follow. Until then, investors are likely to remain cautious, limiting any significant upside in the near-term.

Financial Performance

Despite their issues, the Dividend Aristocrat was able to again raise full-year guidance. And this was led by their strong investment activity during the quarter.

NNN invested $283 million at a cap rate of 7.3%. Dispositions totaled 23 properties, 11 of these vacant at an average cap rate of 5.9%, achieving a healthy spread above 100 basis points.

This netted them $41 million in proceeds. Through the first 3 quarters, NNN has invested $750 million at an average cap rate of 7.4%. As a result of higher investment activity, they were able to beat on both top & bottom-line estimates.

Core FFO amounted to $0.85, beating estimates by $0.01 while revenue of $230.16 million beat by $1.29 million. Year-over their top line rose a solid 5.3% while core FFO increased a penny. AFFO amounted to $0.86 during the quarter and rose 2.4% year-over-year.

While NNN's performance is nothing spectacular, managing to see bottom line growth amidst tenant headwinds is a testament to their overall fundamentals.

Guidance

As previously mentioned, NNN raised full-year guidance with core FFO now expected in a range of $3.36 - $3.40. This is up from $3.34 - $3.39 previously. AFFO is now expected to be between $3.41 - $3.45, up a penny on the lower end.

Using their annual dividend estimate, this gives NNN a very healthy payout ratio of 68.5%. This gives NNN plenty of retained cash flow for future acquisitions. And their strong cash flow is likely the reason they also raised acquisition and disposition guidance during Q3.

Acquisitions are now projected to be $850 million - $950 million, up from $600 million to $700 million previously. Dispositions are expected between $170 million to $200 million, up from $120 million to $150 million.

Management stated free cash flow after dividends was $48 million. So, despite the temporary decline in occupancy and macro uncertainty, NNN's yield near 6% remains well-covered.

Valuation & Risks

At their current price of $41, this gives NNN a forward P/AFFO ratio of just 11.95x. For a REIT of NNN's quality, I consider them just as good as Agree Realty (ADC) and Realty Income (O).

However, I think the market punishes NNN for their preference to focus on non-investment rated tenants vs higher quality tenants like Agree Realty.

While this does make them a riskier play with less upside potential, NNN's long history of navigating market conditions warrants a higher multiple in my opinion.

For comparison, Agree Realty's forward multiple is the highest at 17.04x while Realty Income's sits at 13.29x. Using the sector median's 15.26x, this puts NNN's fair value in the $52 - $53 range.

For the near to medium-term, I do suspect any significant upside may be limited and expect them to trade rangebound for the time being. Additionally, the recent job losses could continue into 2026, limiting upside as recession fears rise.

And this would likely have a higher impact on NNN vs peers like ADC and O due to their higher-quality tenants. Of the group, I expect Agree Realty to see less downside due to their near 70% investment-rated tenant exposure.

If we do see a recession, NNN's could see further declines in its occupancy if tenants see downward financial pressure that forces more bankruptcies.

Tenant At Home was able to successfully emerge from Chapter 11 last month and re-affirmed 100% of their leases.

But further economic weakening could impact the REIT going forward. And while volatility may ensue, resulting in a price pullback in the $30's, long-term investors should view this as an opportunity.

Bottom Line

NNN REIT reported a solid Q3 and was able to raise full-year guidance amidst tenant issues causing occupancy to experience a decline. I view this as temporary and think the price pullback is a buying opportunity for long-term investors.

While I do suspect significant upside may be limited due to their current issues and the potential to see further economic weakening, NNN's dividend and valuation remain attractive.

Moreover, they are likely to see tailwinds from lower base rates in the next year or two, which could result in strong upside. If you're a long-term investor, you should view NNN's current headwinds as short-term noise and collect a well-covered dividend yield near 6% while you wait.

data.title: MercadoLibre: The Recent Dip Is A Gift
data.description:
Introduction

MercadoLibre (MELI) continues to deliver extremely solid operating results across all of its divisions, despite the fact that profitability has slightly decreased as of late. While the stock has declined by over 9.5% since my previous report on this stock, I remain confident in the long-term growth thesis for the business.

The company continues to increase its user base rapidly, and adoption of the firm's fintech offerings (such as the lending solutions) is really beginning to take off. Therefore, despite the recent weakness in the share price and a couple of notable headwinds, I am reiterating my STRONG BUY rating on MercadoLibre.

Business Description & Key Considerations

MercadoLibre is a massive e-commerce and fintech company that is based in Latin America and operates across 18 different countries with a cumulative population of 650 million people.

The Marketplace platform is the driving force of the business, as it allows third-party sellers to list goods across a wide array of product categories for buyers to purchase.

Mercado Envios is the firm's logistics solution, which helps sellers on the Marketplace platform transport goods, which enables discounted or even free shipping to buyers, a key leg up over many other e-commerce players in the region. This logistics network is centered around MercadoLibre's fulfillment centers and vast transportation network, both of which enable the seamless movement of products.

Mercado Ads is a service the company offers that gives sellers access to the vast amount of first party consumer data MercadoLibre has been able to collect over many years.

Through this service, brands can target their ideal consumer demographics through product ads on the Marketplace platform directly, or through third party websites if advertisers wish to target potential customers who may not be using MercadoLibre's applications often.

Mercado Pago is the firm's digital payments solution, which allows consumers to easily pay merchants for goods purchased through the Marketplace. Over time, Mercado Pago has evolved substantially to allow users to also send money to friends and family, pay bills, or even make in-app purchases on mobile games. MercadoLibre also lets consumers pay in equal installments for purchases made using this service, which helps the company generate additional fee income.

MercadoLibre has also created its own lending solution, which is available across the firm's three largest target markets, with those being Brazil, Mexico, and Argentina.

The company offers loans and credit cards to consumers (as well as merchants) who are already a part of the company's established ecosystem, which is absolutely brilliant since the company is able to review their own proprietary data on these users to see how likely it is that they will be able to pay off loans or credit card balances.

Also, these credit products further reinforce the closed-loop ecosystem the firm has built, as consumers will take out loans or use their MercadoLibre branded credit cards to purchase goods through the Marketplace platform, while using the Mercado Pago payment systems to process the transaction. This is becoming a more significant business line, with the credit portfolio hitting $11 billion by the end of the third quarter (up 83% YoY).

Finally, MercadoLibre offers an asset management product to users across its core markets, which strengthens the competitive positioning of the company against some of the large banks that operate in the region. MercadoLibre gives users the option to open high yield savings accounts or invest in cryptocurrencies, which again helps to bolster the firm's stance within the region as a one stop shop for all digital finance needs.

The other massive benefit of offering fintech services while simultaneously running an e-commerce platform is that it allows consumers to seamlessly take funds from their savings and spend them on goods through the Marketplace platform. The company also highly incentivizes consumers and merchants to adopt and use its Mercado Pago payment solutions, which means the company is part of the entire transaction process from end to end.

On a related note, I believe that one of the largest tailwinds for the business moving forward will be enabling users to deposit their paychecks directly through Mercado Pago. The benefits here are numerous, as users will become even more financially dependent on the platform, which will naturally increase usage of all of MercadoLibre's services.

The biggest obstacle for the company that stands in the way of accomplishing this at scale will be obtaining its banking license in Mexico, as regulators require the firm to have this before they enable this feature. MercadoLibre already allows users in Brazil to use direct deposit; however, adoption of this tool remains minimal so far.

Now let's turn to some developments that were highlighted during the company's most recent quarterly earnings call. First off, MercadoLibre has begun to wind down its Mercado Shops service and has introduced Mi Página as a substitute to take its place. Mercado Shops previously allowed sellers to create their own digital storefronts, in a similar manner to Shopify (SHOP).

MercadoLibre is now forcing sellers to build these online stores directly inside the Marketplace platform, which will undoubtedly strengthen the overall ecosystem as fewer consumers will be traveling to other websites outside the core platform.

This initiative should not only improve the company's ability to collect first party customer data, but it should also allow MercadoLibre to increase monetization of each user by delivering more ads and upselling its credit products for consumers who don't have the requisite funds to purchase goods at the time of sale.

MercadoLibre has also recently started to test a new search algorithm on the Marketplace platform that will automatically identify which sellers are offering the best overall value for consumers and rank their listings accordingly. This algorithm will take into account the price of each seller's goods relative to other similar offerings, as well as other factors like delivery speed and the customer experience inside each unique digital storefront.

The strategic rationale here is relatively straightforward in my view, since competition is increasing rapidly throughout MercadoLibre's target markets and the company is looking to reward its merchants who offer the best user experience. This should improve the Marketplace platform offering over time, which will likely lead to further increases in gross merchandise value [GMV] and the number of items purchased per user.

On a similar note, the company hit 76.8 million total unique buyers in Q3, with around 7.8 million of those being completely new to using MercadoLibre's services. The company has been investing a substantial amount of its resources in acquiring younger users through its affiliate marketing channel, which essentially refers to marketing through third-party partners like social media influencers.

As of right now, marketing expenses are expected to remain elevated and are likely going to continue scaling as sales continue to grow so that the firm can continue capturing market share. So far these marketing efforts have led to a substantial increase in the number of items sold per user, with this metric hitting 8.3 in the most recent quarter (up 11% YoY).

One of the most important catalysts for the business moving forward is the recently established partnership with Casas Bahia, who will now be selling its large appliances through the Marketplace platform.

I believe that this is important because MercadoLibre has historically lagged competitors in offering these types of products, and the company has a limited number of merchants on its platform who specialize in B2B sales. Most businesses need appliances such as refrigerators and other pieces of furniture for their offices, so this deal should allow MercadoLibre to begin servicing a new subset of larger, better capitalized customers.

Finally, Argentina has become a spot of worry due to the challenging macroeconomic backdrop tied to the recent midterm elections in the country. Revenue growth for this portion of the business slowed slightly in Q3, and higher interest rates increased costs for MercadoLibre while also having a tangible impact on discretionary spending by consumers.

Despite these headwinds, the company managed to double its loan book YoY among Argentinian consumers, and there were minimal increases in non-performing loans (NPLs) despite both higher rates and the broader uncertainty that is present across the nation.

Financials & Valuation

MercadoLibre posted strong third-quarter results overall, with revenue growing 39.5% YoY to $7.4 billion and operating income hitting $724 million (up 30% YoY).

Total commerce revenue, which includes contributions from the Marketplace, Mercado Envios, and Mercado Ads businesses, grew 33% YoY to approximately $4.2 billion.

This was largely due to a 28% increase in gross merchandise value (GMV), along with an increase in the amount of lower-priced items purchased by consumers, which MercadoLibre charges flat fees on to offset higher per-unit shipping costs.

On the other hand, fintech revenue (which is made up of sales related to the Mercado Pago, asset management, and lending divisions) grew 48.9% YoY to nearly $2.2 billion. This massive increase was attributable to a substantial uptick in loan originations, as well as a 41% increase in total payment volume (TPV).

Looking at the results disaggregated by geography, Mexico saw sales grow 44.2% YoY to $1.65 billion, with revenue in Argentina and Brazil rising 39.5% and 37.6% YoY to $1.4 billion and $4 billion, respectively.

While the aforementioned figures paint a rosy picture, the firm's contribution margin did fall by approximately 350bps YoY to 18.8% as both the Brazil and Argentina segments saw sharp declines in profitability due to higher costs. In both nations MercadoLibre experienced higher shipping and carrier costs, along with increased funding costs related to the fintech side of the business.

Over time I think that MercadoLibre should be able to offset most of these cost increases by both scaling and increasing profitability in the lending business as credit card cohorts mature and consumers continue to pay off their existing loan balances. Therefore, I, personally, don't view this deterioration in margins as a massive headwind for the company.

Turning to the lending business, MercadoLibre's net interest margin after losses (NIMAL) decreased by 200bps sequentially and 320bps YoY. However, the number of NPLs actually improved across the board with fewer bad loans as a percentage of the company's entire portfolio.

The main driver behind the decrease in the firm's NIMAL was the aforementioned increase in funding costs (specifically in Brazil and Argentina). The profitability of this business unit is expected to remain somewhat volatile over the next few years as the company continues to issue new credit cards to customers in the less mature Mexico and Argentina markets.

On the bright side, many cohorts in Brazil have already reached breakeven for the company, given that MercadoLibre originally launched its credit card business in this nation.

Finally, MercadoLibre increased its capital expenditures by over 60% YoY in Q3 FY25, totaling around $357 million for the quarter. The primary areas of investment continue to be in the e-commerce and logistics arms of the business, with a massive 41% YoY increase in fulfillment center capacity.

These capital deployments are absolutely necessary as the company is still increasing the number of items sold, especially following the firm's decision to lower its free shipping threshold for goods sold in Brazil (which led to an 8% QoQ reduction in shipping costs for MercadoLibre).

Despite the increase in CapEx, the company managed to increase adjusted free cash flow (FCF) by around $400 million YoY to $206 million. However, this figure did decline sequentially due to a smaller cash inflow related to fintech funding.

Moving on, to determine the intrinsic value of shares of MercadoLibre, I have created a discounted cash flow [DCF] model. The perpetual growth rate I used in the model was 3%, and my projection covers the next 10 years.

For my cost of capital, I first computed the cost of debt using the TTM interest expense, average debt, and tax rate information that's publicly available. Next, I calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. Treasury bond as my risk-free rate, the stock's beta listed on Yahoo Finance, and the equity risk premium from Professor Aswath Damodaran's website.

Below are my calculations for both the weighted-average cost of capital [WACC] and my DCF model:

My revenue growth rates were roughly in line with consensus estimates, and given the lower NIMAL figures along with higher shipping and marketing expenses, I have operating margins declining slightly over the next few years before picking up again as I expect the business to benefit from continued scale.

For the cash flow statement items, I have modeled depreciation to remain constant at 3.5% and CapEx to be between 3.5% and 4.5%, given the firm's ongoing logistics infrastructure investments.

Based on all of these assumptions, I arrived at an equity value of ~$142.3 billion or an implied share price of $2,806.47. This represents substantial upside of around 36.4% based off the closing share price as of November 17th, 2025.

Conclusion

Despite the recent margin pressure MercadoLibre has experienced, I believe that the fundamental growth story remains well intact. My updated DCF model still indicates extremely solid upside potential, even after I lowered operating margins compared to my previous analysis.

While my initial rating on the stock has not proven out yet, I believe the recent weakness opens up an attractive buying opportunity for investors who have not bought into this name yet, and I am maintaining my STRONG BUY rating.

data.title: The Gabelli Convertible And Income Securities Fund Inc. Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Objective

The Gabelli Convertible and Income Securities Fund (GCV) is a diversified, closed-end management investment company whose primary investment objective is to seek a high level of total return through a combination of current income and capital appreciation.

Fund Performance

Gabelli Convertible and Income Securities Fund significantly outperformed the ICE BAML All Convertibles Index for the quarter with returns of 15.0% relative to the index at 8.6%.

Convertibles were a top performing asset class in the quarter, driven by strong underlying equity performance. data.title: French Business Sentiment Lifted By Services
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

The French business climate indicator rose to 98 from 97 in October, according to Insee. The improvement is largely due to services, where confidence gained 3 points on more favourable expectations for demand and activity. The PMI survey, also released this morning, confirms the trend: the services index hit its highest level in 15 months, signalling a cyclical rebound.

By contrast, industry is flashing warning signs. The manufacturing PMI fell to its lowest in nine months, and the business climate reflects weaker order books and production prospects. Transport equipment firms are particularly downbeat after a strong growth phase. Momentum in this sector appears to be fading, pointing to a weaker industrial contribution from Q4 onwards and into 2026. While some cyclical improvement in certain industrial sub-sectors is likely, the slowdown in transport will weigh on overall industrial growth. Confidence in retail also slipped slightly but remains well above September levels.

Limited growth expected in 2026

Overall, the French economy looks uneven: industry, notably aerospace, is losing steam, while services benefit from renewed confidence. This uptick, helped by political calm despite the absence of a 2026 budget, could support consumption in the fourth quarter. However, the recovery rests on fragile foundations and may fade quickly.

Fiscal uncertainty will remain a major drag. We expect no budget agreement before year-end, meaning the 2025 budget will roll over into 2026. Fiscal tightening will then be introduced gradually, but it is unlikely the deficit will fall to 4.6% of GDP as promised by the government (5% seems more realistic, after 5.4% in 2025). A more restrictive fiscal stance will cap French growth, which should stay below the eurozone average: we forecast GDP growth of 0.9% in 2026 after 0.8% in 2025, compared with 1.1% for the eurozone (after 1.4% in 2025).

In short, while the rebound in services offers temporary relief, tighter fiscal policy signals a moderate trajectory for the French economy.

"Global actual cobalt feedstock supply will drop to 198,000t of metal equivalent in 2025, down by 38pc from 2024, due to the DRC's cobalt export restrictions, although feedstock production reached 288,000t of metal equivalent during the period.
  • Suppliers to China's electric-vehicle battery makers are pushing for higher prices for key cobalt-based materials, aided by a sharp rebound in cobalt and a tightening market for raw materials in batteries.
  • CMOC Group's 2026 DRC export quota is 31,200t. Unofficial reports suggest the DRC government is targeting a floor price of $60,000/t, cobalt currently trades at around $45,000/t on the LME (as of late Oct. 2025).
  • Glencore's 2026 DRC export quota is 18,800t.
  • Huayou Cobalt DRC subsidiary Congo Dongfang International Mining -Congo suspends activities at Chinese mine after spill.
  • GEM Co issues its first tech innovation-themed medium-term note.
  • Sumitomo Metal Mining's Fiscal H1 profit jumps 16%.
  • Sherritt International Q3 adjusted net loss from continuing operations was $15.5 million...
  • Nickel 28 - Ramu Q3 Cobalt sales of 948t of contained cobalt, compared to 798t in the same period last year.
  • Electra reactivates construction at Cobalt Sulfate Refinery in North America, targeted commissioning in 2027.
  • Sunrise Energy Metals - Successful A$46m placement to fund pre-construction activities at Syerston Scandium Project.
  • Cobalt Blue - Advancing Black Mass recycling at the Broken Hill Technology Centre.
  • Havilah Resources and Sandfire Resources agree to a A$210M (scrip & cash) binding deal to advance the Kalkaroo Copper-Gold Project and regional exploration.
  • Alliance Nickel-Stellantis Offtake Agreement to be renegotiated.
  • GEMC announces termination of Luna Energy transaction.
  • The Metals Corp. announces a net loss of $20.5 million for the quarter ended September 30, 2024.
  • Chilean Cobalt Corp. enters Offtake Arrangement with Glencore on La Cobaltera and El Cofre Projects.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Provident Financial Services: I'm Not Ready To Stop Banking On Upside
data.description:
To be honest with you, I am a little disappointed with how shares of Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) have performed as of late. Back in August of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was after the stock had performed nicely compared to the broader market and was driven in part by the completion of its merger with Lakeland Bancorp. Even though revenue and profits have risen nicely, and even though the size of the firm's balance sheet has gotten larger, shares have underperformed the market, inching up by only 0.9% while the S&P 500 is up 6.2%. I guess the good news is that, compared to when I initially rated the firm a ‘buy’ back in February of last year, the stock has performed better, rising by 32.5%. But even so, that does fall short of the 37.2% eyes that the S&P 500 saw.

Looking at the picture today, I see a business that's continuing to achieve strong year-over-year growth. The aforementioned merger certainly helped to boost overall assets. And on top of this, asset quality remains high while shares are cheap relative to earnings and book value. When you add all of these things together, it's difficult not to be bullish about this name. So although the market has disagreed with my assessment as of late, I believe that maintaining the business as a ‘buy’ candidate is the right choice.

Still banking on Provident Financial Services

The newest data that we have regarding Provident Financial Services covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. This is only one additional operating quarter compared to when I last analyzed the bank. During the quarter, deposits for the institution came in at $19.10 billion. That was up nicely from the $18.62 billion the company had on its books at the end of last year. This is great to see in and of itself. However, all of this deposit growth and more came from high-cost brokered deposits that the institution got from other financial organizations. That metric grew from $255 million at the end of last year to $805.9 million in the third quarter of this year. This helped to offset weakness in savings accounts and, to a lesser extent, in non-interest-bearing deposit accounts. But in general, I'm not a fan of seeing such a big move higher in brokered deposits.

One other thing that's worth mentioning when it comes to deposits would be exposure to uninsured deposits. I typically like to see this metric come in at 30% of total deposits and sure enough, when we strip out collateralized uninsured deposits, we get exposure amounting to 25.2%. This means to me that the institution has a lower risk profile than it otherwise would if uninsured deposits were higher. After all, the higher that uninsured deposits are, the higher the risk is during a crisis that a bank run will occur.

The value of loans in the most recent quarter came in at $19.10 billion. That represented a nice move higher over the $18.63 billion the company ended last year with. The greatest exposure that Provident Financial Services has when it comes to loans would be the $7.32 billion of commercial loans on its books. That accounts for approximately 37.9% of its entire loan portfolio. Outside of this, commercial loans amounted to $4.84 billion, while multifamily loans came in at $3.53 billion. One thing I will say is that whenever I see a lot of exposure to commercial loans, specifically commercial real estate like the $10.98 billion worth of the company has on its books, I am always worried about exposure to office assets because of high and rising vacancy rates. But the good news is that only 4.1% of its entire loan portfolio is dedicated to these types of assets. That is quite low and puts the company near the lowest of the banks that I look at.

We should also pay attention to other aspects of the balance sheet. During the quarter, the company had securities amounting to $3.57 billion. That was up from the $3.12 billion reported a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, jumped from $205.9 million to $301.6 million. And finally, debt on its books grew, but only a bit, climbing from $2.42 billion to $2.61 billion.

When it comes to the income statement, I am happy to say that we have seen some nice growth year over year. The changes in the size of the company's balance sheet, combined with a rise in its net interest margin from 3.31% to 3.43%, took its net interest income up from $174.4 million in the third quarter of last year to $187.3 million at the same time this year. One thing that also helped here was a decline in its provision for credit losses from $9.3 million down to only $7 million. And for the first nine months of this year, these same improvements pushed net interest income up to $558.4 million compared to the $340.2 million reported last year.

Non-interest income for the institution has also been growing. For the most recent quarter, this amounted to $27.4 million, which represented a small increase over the $26.9 million reported a year earlier. Surprisingly, wealth management income actually contracted from $7.6 million to $7.3 million. But even with that, things would have been better had it not been for a decline in bank-owned life insurance revenue from $4.3 million to $2.7 million. The improvements to offset this involved fees revenue, insurance agency income, and other miscellaneous income, all rising year over year. A similar trend can be seen when looking at the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year, with an increase from $69.9 million to $81.5 million.

That ultimately pushed net profits up significantly from $67 million to $207.7 million. And for the most recent quarter on its own, the $71.7 million generated by the company meaningfully outperformed the $46.4 million reported last year. It is worth noting that some, in fact a lot of the improvement in profitability here came from merger-related expenses last year that were not repeated this year. That included $15.6 million in the third quarter, with that number growing to $36.7 million for the first nine months of last year compared to this year.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get a price-to-earnings multiple for the institution of 8.6. As the chart above illustrates, this puts the company cheaper than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. It's also comfortably below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. The good news is that, on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis, the stock is cheap compared to those of banks. In the chart below, you can see that only one of the five firms is cheaper than it is using these metrics.

On top of boasting low trading multiples and improving fundamentals, another thing that Provident Financial Services has going for it is the fact that asset quality appears robust. In the first chart below, you can see that the firm has a return on assets reading of 1.16%. That's higher than the 1% minimum I like to see, and it puts the company around the middle of the group when talking about the aforementioned firms. Its ranking is similar when using its return on equity, which is shown in the next chart below. The 10.39% that we get here is above the 10% minimum I prefer. And three of the five institutions are lower than our candidate on this basis.

Takeaway

Right now, I think that things are going pretty well for Provident Financial Services. The company is doing a solid job. Revenue, profits, and balance sheet items are all mostly getting better. Net interest margin is expanding, and asset quality is really good. When you add all of these things together, it's difficult not to rate the enterprise a ‘buy’ at this time.
